content.text:
Question #1Write function prototypes for the following cases. Use const if/when necessary.a) A function named max() that takes two doubles and returns the larger of the two.Show Solutiondouble max(double x, double y);b) A function named swap() that swaps two integers.Show Solutionvoid swap(int& x, int& y);c) A function named getLargestElement() that takes a dynamically allocated array of integers and returns the largest number in such a way that the caller can change the value of the element returned (don’t forget the length parameter).Show Solution// Note: array can't be const in this case, because returning a non-const reference to a const element would be a const violation.
int& getLargestElement(int* array, int length);

content.code:
// Note: array can't be const in this case, because returning a non-const reference to a const element would be a const violation.
int& getLargestElement(int* array, int length);

content.text: doSomething() returns a reference to a local variable that will be destroyed when doSomething terminates.
function sumTo () has no termination condition. Variable value will eventually go negative, and the function will loop infinitely until the stack overflows.
The two divide functions are not distinct, as they have the same name and same parameters. There is also a potential divide by 0 issue.
The array is too large to be allocated on the stack. It should be dynamically allocated.
Show Solutionargv[1] may not exist. If it does, argv[1] is a string argument, and can’t be converted to an integer via assignment.
argv[1] may not exist. If it does, argv[1] is a string argument, and can’t be converted to an integer via assignment.

list: Look at the center element of the array (if the array has an even number of elements, round down).If the center element is greater than the target element, discard the top half of the array (or recurse on the bottom half)If the center element is less than the target element, discard the bottom half of the array (or recurse on the top half).If the center element equals the target element, return the index of the center element.If you discard the entire array without finding the target element, return a sentinel that represents “not found” (in this case, we’ll use -1, since it’s an invalid array index).

If the center element is greater than the target element, discard the top half of the array (or recurse on the bottom half)If the center element is less than the target element, discard the bottom half of the array (or recurse on the top half).If the center element equals the target element, return the index of the center element.If you discard the entire array without finding the target element, return a sentinel that represents “not found” (in this case, we’ll use -1, since it’s an invalid array index).

If the center element is less than the target element, discard the bottom half of the array (or recurse on the top half).If the center element equals the target element, return the index of the center element.If you discard the entire array without finding the target element, return a sentinel that represents “not found” (in this case, we’ll use -1, since it’s an invalid array index).

Pass 1) We calculate the midpoint of min (0) and max (8), which is 4. Element #4 has value 12, which is larger than our target value. Because the array is sorted, we know that all elements with index equal to or greater than the midpoint (4) must be too large. So we leave min alone, and set max to 3.Pass 2) We calculate the midpoint of min (0) and max (3), which is 1. Element #1 has value 6, which is smaller than our target value. Because the array is sorted, we know that all elements with index equal to or lesser than the midpoint (1) must be too small. So we set min to 2, and leave max alone.Pass 3) We calculate the midpoint of min (2) and max (3), which is 2. Element #2 has value 7, which is our target value. So we return 2.

Pass 2) We calculate the midpoint of min (0) and max (3), which is 1. Element #1 has value 6, which is smaller than our target value. Because the array is sorted, we know that all elements with index equal to or lesser than the midpoint (1) must be too small. So we set min to 2, and leave max alone.Pass 3) We calculate the midpoint of min (2) and max (3), which is 2. Element #2 has value 7, which is our target value. So we return 2.
Pass 3) We calculate the midpoint of min (2) and max (3), which is 2. Element #2 has value 7, which is our target value. So we return 2.

content.text: Tipstd::binary_searchreturns true if a value exists in a sorted list.std::equal_rangereturns the iterators to the first and last element with a given value.Don’t use these functions to solve the quiz, but use them in the future if you need a binary search.
